answers.answer: Perception, memory, logic, and coordination. The book states (ch3§5¶10): 'The core technologies involved combine perception, memory, logic, and coordination to create the foundation for collective intelligence in agentic systems' — combining contextual awareness with situational reasoning.

Model Context Protocol (MCP), Agent Network Protocol (ANP), Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol, and Agent Communication Protocol (ACP). The book (ch3§2¶11) describes these as the four lightweight protocols the industry is currently leaning toward.

ANP stands for Agent Network Protocol. It solves the problem of enabling secure multi-agent collaboration across organizations and domains by providing identity, authentication, and trust mechanisms — often using decentralized identifiers (DIDs) — so agents can discover one another, share tasks or data, and coordinate while maintaining verifiable provenance and privacy boundaries. Its differentiator vs MCP is sustained decentralization / peer-to-peer structure with no central authority.

MCP was created by Anthropic (described as 'AI safety and research company Anthropic') and open-sourced in late 2024. The book's analogy is that MCP 'acts like a USB-C cable for AI: a universal connector that allows different AI systems to plug into tools, apps, and data sources without needing a custom setup each time' (also described as a 'USB-C port for AI').

Title: 'The Illusion of Thinking: Understanding the Strengths and Limitations of Reasoning Models via the Lens of Problem Complexity' (Apple 2025). Central claim: today's reasoning models don't actually reason — they replay patterns from training data; they look brilliant on simple/familiar tasks but their 'thinking' breaks down as problems get harder or move out of distribution. True reasoning (cause/effect, spotting breaks in logic, adapting on the fly) is something these models still can't do.

Two examples named (ch2§0¶7-12): (a) LangChain — a framework that helps AI agents interact with external data by 'chains of reasoning,' chaining together multiple steps/components to accomplish complex tasks requiring sequential processing and decision-making. (b) OpenAI function calling — lets agents access and retrieve live data from external APIs by defining functions in code (e.g., a weather chatbot calling a weather API). AutoGen is also named alongside them as a third example.

Hierarchical planning and goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (ch3§0¶44). Hierarchical planning decomposes large tasks into subgoals when a problem is too big for a single agent (e.g., a delivery drone breaking a long route into smaller waypoints). Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning lets agents adjust plans when environments or other agents behave differently than expected — reassigning priorities or modifying strategies via what-if reasoning so the team still hits the overall goal (e.g., an autonomous drone simulating different flight paths in milliseconds to avoid bad weather and conserve battery).

Chapter 12 recommends the 70-20-10 learning model adapted for the agentic age (70% on-the-job experience, 20% learning from others/social, 10% formal structured learning). Figure 12-1's caption states: 'A learning feedback loop system embedded in the 70-20-10 learning model' — i.e., a continuous feedback loop is embedded inside the 70-20-10 structure to keep learning intentional and aligned with changing job needs.

Swarm Agentic AI is defined (ch13§3¶46) as 'a distributed system that comprises multiple autonomous agents acting independently but working together toward a single goal' — networks of coordinated autonomous agents without a central control mechanism. Inspiration cited (ch13§4¶7): natural systems such as ant colonies and bee hives — featuring distributed intelligence, dynamic coordination, and emergent behavior that exceeds what individual agents could do.

NIST's AI Risk Management Framework (in the U.S.) and ISO's AI Management System Standard (ISO/IEC 42001). The book (ch7§0¶50) explicitly states: 'Governance frameworks seek to resolve this issue by making responsibility explicit and non-transferable. NIST's AI Risk Management Framework in the U.S. and ISO's AI Management System Standard (ISO/IEC 42001) both stress that humans, whether they're regulators, institutions, or operators, remain accountable for system outcomes.'

verdict.visual_pleasure: Typography is calm and well-tuned for long-form reading — ink-blue accent, serif body, generous measure, drop caps at chapter openers, dark-mode support. But two persistent issues hold this to a 3: (a) the article body is dominated by raw <blockquote class='anchor'> stacks rather than condensed prose — many chapters read as an annotated source extract rather than a written-through condensation, which kills scrolling momentum; (b) several h3 section headings are mid-sentence fragments (e.g. 'Allow agents (in newer systems) to communicate by using more' / 'natural, human-readable language (known as natural language'), visually jarring at chapter scale. The chrome is polished; the body text is not.
verdict.navigation: Sticky left TOC with chapter-level + by-theme grouping is genuinely useful, the top progress bar and percent-read indicator work, scroll-spy highlights the active chapter, and source-ID anchors on every blockquote let you trace any claim back to its source span. The mid-sentence h3 fragments fragment the scroll-spy state slightly but don't break it. Solid 4.
verdict.comprehension: Answered 10/10 with high confidence from the HTML alone. The four protocols (MCP/ANP/A2A/ACP) are named in full multiple times, MCP's Anthropic+2024+USB-C analogy is intact, the Apple 2025 study title is verbatim including subtitle, the 70-20-10 model + embedded feedback loop is fully rendered (including the pie-chart figure), the NIST RMF + ISO/IEC 42001 accountability pairing is a single clean sentence in ch7, and the swarm definition + ant colony / bee hive inspiration are both present. Hits the 5 band cleanly.
verdict.illustration: 55 figures: ~30 mermaid diagrams (concept flows, decision trees, the protocol scaling problem, the alignment cascade, the 70-20-10 pie, the agentic-skills stack) plus per-chapter Pollinations hero images. The mermaid diagrams genuinely externalize relationships (e.g., 'why APIs strain at multi-agent scale' makes the ANP/A2A/MCP motivation click instantly; the flash-crash cascade visualizes the failure mode). Captions cite source IDs, so each figure is anchored to a specific claim. Heroes are atmospheric but generic. 4.
verdict.books_finished_rate: Yes — I would skip the original. Every named entity I'd want from a Dummies-level briefing on agentic AI is in here: the four protocols with creators and analogies, the Apple study with its central claim, the named frameworks (LangChain/AutoGen/function calling), the planning techniques (hierarchical planning + goal-conditioned RL), the governance frameworks (NIST RMF + ISO/IEC 42001 + EU AI Act + OECD), the swarm definition with biological analogies, and the 70-20-10 upskilling model with embedded feedback loops. The condensation isn't elegant — it's blockquote-heavy and the Editor clearly punted on rewriting many spans — but the SUBSTANCE is complete and comprehensible. For 80% of working-knowledge purposes I would trust this over reading the original. 4. (Would be a 5 if the Editor had rewritten the blockquote stacks into proper condensed prose.)

notes_on_prior_iteration: Prior pre-doctype dump bug fixed; HTML now opens with a single clean <!doctype html> + <html> + <head> + <body> + <article>, no leaked pre-doctype text. Re-scored against the repaired 677 KB artifact. Remaining weakness is editorial (blockquote-heavy substrate, mid-sentence h3 fragments), not structural — does NOT block the gate. All five thresholds met: visual>=3, navigation>=3, comprehension>=3, illustration>=3, books_finished_rate>=4.
